need   to   thank   rofessor   hristoph   arkschies   for   his   kindness   in
furnishing me in advance of publication with a copy of the   ortregister   to
oll’s   anarion .  must also thank err riedrich-hristian ollatz, our
eries editor tephen mmel, and vo omein and llen irmscheid of
rill for their understanding and support, and my daughter arge for her
important technical help.

his translation of ook  of piphanius’   anarion   was originally published
in  and was reprinted with a few changes in . emand for it has
been suf fi cient to warrant a second edition, which is offered here. he
opportunity has been taken to review and revise the translation, edit and
epand the notes and inde and add indices of references. ogether with
its companion volume,   he anarion of piphanius of alamis, ooks  and ,
e ide   () this is the only current version in a modern language of the
 anarion   in its entirety. 
everal works of signi fi cance have appeared since . line ourkier’s
important   ’héresiologie chez Épiphane de alamine   () carries further the
study of the   anarion ’s sources which was begun in the nineteenth century
by ichard ipsius, and also analyzes piphanius’ ways of dealing with his
data. ourkier tests ipsius’ conclusions on ten ects, seven of them from
ook . n the course of her study she translates etensively from the   anarion
 and from piphanius’ predecessors renaeus and “seudo-ertullian” and
his younger contemporary, ilaster of rescia.
hilip midon’s   he anarion of t. piphanius of alamis, elected assages
 () could be termed a modern epitome of the   anarion . t renders those
passages which describe sects but omits piphanius’ refutations. midon’s
rendition amounts in all to about two   fi  fths of the work.
on echow’s   ogma and ysticism in arly hristianity, piphanius of yprus
and the egacy of rigen   appeared in book form in  although it had
previously been available on micro fi lm. echow translates ecerpts from
piphanius only incidentally but provides a penetrating study of his life, the
anti-rigenist aspect of this thought, and the rigenist controversies of his
last years.
inally, of etreme importance to students of piphanius is the 
publication of oll’s   ortregister   to the   anarion   through the good of fi ces
of riedrich-hristian ollatz, hristoph arkschies and other scholars.
waited for nearly a century, this invaluable tool also includes grammatical
and subject indices, and besides facilitating the study of the   anarion   should
make practicable the revision of oll’s critical tet.
   nineteenth century ussian version eists but is rare, and long out of print.

he present work has been compared   with all of these and is indebted to
them all.
 et
 e render arl oll’s critical tet of the   anarion ’s ook . he   fi rst  ects
are from oll’s   ncoratus, anarion ook    which was issued, with achapparat
and tetual notes, in . he rest are from oll’s second volume,
republished in  by ürgen ummer. he  volume includes an
appendi in which ummer assembled suggestions which various scholars
had made for the improvement of oll’s tet. e translate these in the body
of our work, marking them with an asterisk and providing a short appendi
which gives the reek alternatives.
he revision of oll’s tet has often been mooted, but the lack of the
 ortregister   has stood in its way. he enterprise ought now to be practicable.
owever, to revise this enormous tet must be a long drawn out affair
requiring the cooperation of many scholars.
oll published the principles of his treatment of the   anarion ’s tet in .
e concluded that the eleven etant manuscripts, none of them complete,
all descend from a single poorly copied archetype, and that the tet has been
contaminated by atticizing scribes. n the preface to his  edition he
complained that modern editors’ dislike of piphanius had in fl uenced their
view of his tet, that on the one hand they had emended without reference to
his distinctive style and vocabulary, but on the other had allowed absurdities
to stand because piphanius was thought to be “konfus.”
n fact, within his parameters piphanius is a particularly clear thinker.
is reek has its peculiarities but he sets forth his aims and methods clearly
at the outset, carries them through consistently, seldom digresses and returns
to his point when he does, and provides the reader with every help he
can. he dif fi culties of the tet are the results either of scribal error or of
piphanius’ language.
oll’s is a carefully edited critical tet. e sometimes emends, but more
often restores a word or phrase, occasionally a longer unit. is restorations
clear up many dif fi culties and usually appear to be the most logical choice.
ow and then the tet gives a good sense without restoration and the
 anarion , partly written but mostly dictated and that under pressure of time,
may not have been as smooth as oll supposed. onetheless there can be
little doubt that oll has given us a fair approimation of what piphanius
wrote.
ii   introduction

piphanius’ ife and ritings
 ur chief sources of information about piphanius are his own works and
correspondence, references in the writings of his friend erome, in alladius’
 ialogue on the ife of ohn hrysostom , and in asil of aesarea, heophilus
of   leandria, and the histories of   ocrates and ozomen. he short
biographical notice prefaced to ancient editions of piphanius’   ncoratus   is
of doubtful value, as is the legendary life ostensibly by the monks ohn and
olybius.
arly in the fourth century .., perhaps between  and , pi-
phanius was born in alestine at esanduc, a village in the environs of the
city of leutheropolis, near aza. t has been suggested that his parents
were ewish converts to hristianity. n favor of this are the facts that he
was bilingual in reek and yriac and knew a good deal about ewish
hristian sects against it, that his attitude toward ews was antagonistic and
his knowledge of their customs meager.
hat piphanius’ family sent him to gypt in young manhood suggests
that they were well to do. f his   etter to heodosius   is authentic they had
brought him up “in the faith of the fathers of icaea,” and ozomen says
that he received his early education from monks ( ist . .). n important
in fl uence on him was his friend and mentor ilarion, who is credited with
bringing the monastic life to alestine and who in his turn had been taught
by nthony of gypt.
piphanius’ childhood background helps us understand him. ndoctri-
nated in childhood with icene hristianity, he was under monastic
in fl uence in his early years. is education, hristian and scriptural rather
than classical, would have reinforced his childhood training. he homoousian
version of hristianity was crucial to his identity from the   fi rst. t is no
wonder that any rival approach appeared to him as a threat—piphanius
would have termed it a “poisonous snake”—to be repelled at all costs.
piphanius’ destination in gypt would have been the school of a rhetor
in the great city of leandria. ere he had the disturbing encounter with a
seually oriented group whom he identi fi es as nostics and describes in the
 anarion ’s ect . lthough we cannot know this, this episode, dangerous to
his chastity and described by him, even years later, in an emotional manner,
  ited in icephorus   dversus piphanium    , , itra,   picilegium olesmense , p. ,-.
ee oll, “chriften.”
 introduction   iii

might have been a turning point. t the least it helps eplain his detestation
of anything gnostic, and his conviction that all nostics were immoral.
is literary style, or lack of it, shows that he did not complete his rhetorical
training. nstead he joined an gyptian monastic community, where he
remained for some years. nfortunately we do not know which one. iven
his avid reading it must have emphasized knowledge as well as prais. n
the other hand, his virulent anti-rigenism almost guarantees that it took
the anti-rigenist side of the controversy then raging among the monks of
gypt.
eturning to alestine, probably nearer to the age of  than to the 
the preface to the   ncoratus   mentions, piphanius founded a monastery near
leutheropolis and served as its abbot. is friendship with ilarion, whose
monastery was also near aza, continued. erome tells us ( ita ilarionis   )
that when ilarion died piphanius circulated a short work in his praise.
f his years as an abbot we know only his efforts to foster and defend
what he regarded as hristian orthodoy.   anarion   ,,, his only personal
reminiscence of his abbacy, shows him eposing and banishing a nostic
monk. hen in  the bishop of leutheropolis, utychius, signed the
evasive creed of   the ouncil of   eleuceia ( anarion   ,,-,) and
attempted to enforce the homoeousion on his diocese, piphanius was
uncooperative. t was during this period that he visited the homoousian
bishop usebius of ercelli, in eile at iberias, and there met the converted
ew osephus of iberias who told him the colorful story he relates at   anarion
 ,,-,.
ccording to erome ( ontra oannem   ,  ,) piphanius was
instrumental in persuading utychius to change his mind. t has been
suggested, however, that it was the discomfort of the relations between
homoousian abbot and homoeousian bishop which prompted piphanius’
move to yprus—a move which led to his election to the see of alamis
in . 
he clergy of yprus, restive under the patriarchate of ntioch and
inclined to look to leandria for guidance, would have welcomed a
homoousian of wide reputation, a friend of thanasius and an ascetic. he
 anarion   shows so much interest in the monastic life that we must visualize
piphanius, once a bishop, as continuing his own austerity. (e did not
require abstinence from meat and wine, however, and was suspicious of
   con fl  ict with utychius, however, is by no means the only possible reason for pi-
phanius’ move. or various alternatives see echow,   ogma and ysticism .
iv   introduction

leaders who enforced this requirement.) erome’s   ita aulae   tells us that
he fostered the monastic movement, and that his fame attracted novices
from all over the world. e allowed a degree of autonomy to the other
bishops of his far   fl ung province.   ncoratus   - shows us that he was
missionary-minded, eager to convince pagans of   their error and bring
them into the fold. nd though we have no information about his episcopal
administration, the   anarion ’s clear organization and meticulous attention
to detail suggest administrative capacity. t may have been piphanius who
began the construction of the great basilica, the ruins of which still stand
near amagusta.
piphanius’ prestige was great erome refers to him as apa piphanius,
and others may have done the same. he abbots cacius and aul, whose
letter he publishes at the beginning of the   anarion , write, “or not we alone,
but all who hear of you, confess that the avior has raised you up as a
new herald, a new ohn, to proclaim what ought to be observed by those
who resolve on this (monastic) course” ( etter of cacius and aul   ,). t a
time when the rianizing emperor onstantius did not hesitate to eile an
thanasius, the rian emperor alens left piphanius in peace ( erome,
 ontra oannem   .,  ,-). o interfere with him would presumably
have risked an uproar.
piphanius was respected not only for his piety and rectitude but for his
learning. hurches far from yprus consulted him on doctrinal issues. he
 ncoratus , of which we treat below, is his reply to inquiries from the church
at yedra in amphilia. he   etter to rabia   concerning ary’s perpetual
virginity ( anarion   ), is another eample of his responses to queries. t
some time he gathered a collection of etracts from arcion’s canon which
could be used to refute arcion’s thesis he publishes these, together with
his comments on them, in the long   anarion   . “ven in etreme old age,”
erome tells us at   e iris llustribus   , piphanius continued to publish
short works.
is earliest surviving datable work is a fragment of a   etter to usebius,
arcellus, ibianus and arpus , preserved on pages  and  of ode
mbrosianus . his was written somewhere between the years  and
. t defends the ntiochean dating of aster, used by the church on
yprus, on the unday after isan , rather than on the unday after
the spring equino, the leandrian observance.   t includes a chronology
of hrist’s last week on earth which resembles one found in the   postolic
   ee oll,   ruchstück.
 introduction   v

onstitutions , and to which there may be an allusion at ag ammadi’s
 pocryphon of ames   ,-.
rguably piphanius’ best work is the   ncoratus , written in . he
“barque” of the church of yedra, says the introductory correspondence,
cannot   enter   harbor   because   of   contrary   winds   of   wrong   doctrine,
particularly concerning the oly pirit. piphanius shows how a man
can become “anchored” ( etter of alladius   ,   ncoratus   ,). esides
the oly pirit the work discusses the rinity and hrist’s incarnation and
resurrection, attacks the doctrines of rigen, and includes a polemic against
reek religion. t ,-, we   fi nd the outline of what was to become the
 anarion , showing that piphanius already had this work in mind. his was
begun, in fact, in  or  and can be considered a sequel to the   ncoratus .
e discuss it below.
uring this same period, about , piphanius attempted to resolve a
scandalous schism in the important church of ntioch he tells the story
at   anarion   ,,-,.   he hristian community there was divided
into four factions, headed respectively by the rian uzoeus and three
representatives of homoousian hristianity, elitius, italius and aulinus.
elitius had the allegiance of the majority but was in eile.   italius had
been consecrated by pollinarius of aodicea, a respected bishop whose
hristology was, however, suspect. he third, aulinus, had the support
of amasus of ome he was a disciple of the former bishop of ntioch,
ustathius, staunch homoousian and participant in the ouncil of icaea
who, however, had been eiled on a charge of abellianism. nknown to
piphanius the situation was further complicated by italius’ teaching,
learned from pollinarius, that hrist’s mind ( νοῦς ) was not human but
only divine.
piphanius had already encountered distorted forms of this doctrine,
brought to yprus about  by young disciples of pollinarius.   anarion
 ,,- describes some of their ideas and speaks of the calling of a synod to
condemn persons of this kind. n his visit to ntioch piphanius discovered
italius’ adherence to the same doctrine, in a milder form but one which he
still found shocking. hus he could not enter into communion with italius.
or, for reasons we do not know, did he consider communion with elitius.
n the strength of aulinus’ written confession of faith piphanius, and
the ypriote church with him, recognized him as the lawful bishop of
ntioch.
  echow’s   ogma and ysticism   gives a full account of this comple episode.
   anarion   ,,-,. autin, however, doubts that elitius was ever eiled.
vi   introduction

his unfortunately left aulinus’ and italius’ congregations at odds with
each other. piphanius attempted to gain support for aulinus from the
in fl uential asil of aesarea, but to no avail. pollinarius in the meantime
rejected both piphanius and aulinus and consecrated new bishops. he
results of piphanius’ measures at ntioch show both the etent of his
in fl uence and the limitations of it. n the one hand, no one appears to have
resented his intervention in a see not his own but on the other, his word was
by no means always taken as law.
hether piphanius attended the irst ouncil of onstantinople in 
is very doubtful. uring the winter following, however, in , he traveled
to ome with aulinus and erome to attend a synod called by amasus
to discuss the relations between the western and eastern churches. f
piphanius hoped that amasus would af fi rm his earlier support of aulinus
he was disappointed amasus now suspected him of abellianism. uring
this time, however, piphanius boarded with the wealthy widow aula
and was instrumental in persuading her to abandon the luurious life of
a oman aristocrat for the cloister. he journeyed east with erome as her
chaplain and founded a convent at ethlehem ( erome,   ita aulae   ).  few
years later, perhaps in , we   fi nd piphanius visiting her on her sickbed
and laboring, unsuccessfully, to convince her that drinking wine when ill is
proper ( erome,   ita aulae   ).
even years later, in , piphanius published his   e ensuris et onderibus ,
a manual of information for students of scripture n  we   fi nd him on
another visit to alestine, traveling to ethel to share a service with the bishop
of erusalem, ohn. n a village church he found a curtain painted with the
image of hrist or a saint, tore it down at once, and advised the parishioners
to use it as a burial shroud for the poor. is   etter to ohn   (piphanius/erome
 pistle   ) relates the incident and includes piphanius’ promise to replace
the curtain. t also, rather lamely, eplains piphanius’ ordination to the
priesthood of   erome’s brother aulinian—an uncanonical one since,
although it took place at piphanius’ monastery near leutheropolis,
aulinian was to serve at ethlehem, in ohn’s diocese. ost importantly,
however, this   etter   addressed to the convinced rigenist, ohn, is an anti-
rigenist tract and was circulated as such.
piphanius’ war on rigenism and rigenists dominates what we know
of his last years. his had nothing to do with rigen himself, who was long
dead. piphanius admired rigen’s   eapla   and appreciated some of his
writings (cf.   anarion   ,,- and ,-), but considered his doctrine gnostic
and the source of rianism. mportant among his objections, published as
early as the   ncoratus   and repeated in the   anarion , were rigen’s allegedly
subordinationist view of the rinity, his doctrine of the preeistence, fall and
 introduction   vii

restoration of all souls including hrist’s and atan’s, and his denial—or so
piphanius saw it—of the resurrection of the body.
rom  until  piphanius fought against rigenism in erusalem
and alestine. is opponents were ohn of erusalem and u fi nus, almoner
to the abbess elania on the ount of lives and translator into atin of
rigen’s   περὶ   ἀρχῶν . is chief ally was erome.  monk named tarbius,
it is thought at piphanius’ instigation, made the rounds of erusalem’s
monasteries demanding that monks who were suspected of favoring rigen
sign a formal denunciation of   his teachings. erome signed. u fi nus,
predictably, refused to see tarbius.
ither the festival of the ncaenia or the oly eek of  saw an
ugly incident at erusalem. nvited to preach in the morning, piphanius
delivered a denunciation of   rigen which was plainly aimed at ohn.
ohn retorted in the afternoon with a sermon against anthropomorphism,
a view which some monks certainly held and with which rigenists often
stigmatized their accusers.  few days later ohn published a confession of
faith. piphanius could   fi nd no fault with it but, still unsatis fi ed, wrote in
 his   etter to ohn.   his was circularized among the bishops and monks
of alestine, accompanied by another letter which urged them to break
communion with ohn. nstead of   replying ohn wrote an apologia to
heophilus, thanasius’ successor as patriarch of leandria. his in turn
called forth erome’s   ontra oannem   which stated his own version of the case
against rigen. erome also wrote a   ontra u fi num , although he and u fi nus
made peace in .
et followed the crisis of the rigenist controversy in gypt. nder
heavy pressure from anti-rigenist monks, heophilus abandoned his
previous tolerance of   rigenism and proceeded against the rigenist
monks of itria,  miles from leandria. arly in  he convened a
synod which condemned the reading or possession of rigen’s works. his
was followed by a decree of eile for the itrian rigenists, accompanied
by the wrecking of their cells and the burning of their books. heophilus
wrote for support to the churches of alestine and yprus, and in particular
urged piphanius to convene a similar synod on yprus. his he did, and
jubilantly announced the result in a letter to erome (piphanius/erome
 pistle   ).
eanwhile the eiles from itria had made their way to various hristian
and monastic centers. ed by sidore and the distinguished all rothers
(mmonius,   ioscurus,   usebius   and   uthymius)   about      came   to
onstantinople and appealed for help to the patriarch, ohn hrysostom.
viii   introduction

hatever his own attitude toward rigen, hrysostom showed sympathy
for the eiles and wrote to heophilus urging their reinstatement. piphanius
was then moved to set out on what proved to be his last journey, a voyage to
onstantinople for the defense of hristian orthodoy and the unmasking
of ohn’s supposed rigenism.
rriving in the spring of  or , piphanius declined hrysostom’s
offer of hospitality and communion. e, however, held his own service outside
the city and, uncanonically, ordained a deacon. ocrates ( istory   ..-
) and ozomen ( istory   .-) give differing accounts of the subsequent
events. ccording to the later ozomen piphanius had an encounter with
mmonius which convinced him of his own injustice. ocrates, however, says
that while on his way to a public appearance in the athedral of the oly
postles piphanius was confronted by hrysostom’s archdeacon erapion,
who accused him of uncanonical behavior and warned him of the danger
of a riot. hatever the truth of the matter, piphanius left onstantinople
without taking any public action. e died at sea on his way home to yprus.
is refusal to communicate with ohn was used as ammunition by ohn’s
opponents at the ynod of the ak in .
autin has written “l est assurément dommage pour le mémoire
d’Épiphane, que sa dernière intervention dans l’histoire de l’Église ait été
celle-là.” piphanius’ savage harassment of anyone who appeared to approve
of rigen is indeed dif fi cult to stomach. n his defense it may be urged that
he was alestinian, and had also lived for many years in gypt. as he not
defending hearth and home against what he saw as a dangerous virus s
to his support of the rather unsavory heophilus, iggi has reminded us of
his reverence for the see of leandria. ur heresiologist would have been
unlikely to suspect the motives of a successor to thanasius.
piphanius’ writings against images appear to date from the same decade
as the   etter to ohn . his concern makes itself apparent already in the   anarion
 where, at ,,-, he attacks hristian image-making. hree writings
against hristian images can be partially reconstructed from conciliar   acta
 and other sources.   hese are called a   reatise against hose ho, by dolatrous
ustom, re ccustomed to ake mages epresentative of hrist, the other of od

and the artyrs and further, of ngels and rophets  a   etter to heodosius  and a
 estament to the itizens   (of alamis).
nother of   piphanius’ works,   e emmis , also comes from the last
decade of the fourth century. reserved only in a atin epitome, it discusses
the symbolism of the stones in the high priest’s breastplate. t was written
for iodore of arsus and witnesses, both to the close attention with which
piphanius read scripture and to the fact that during this period of his life
he was engaged in other pursuits than an obsessive opposition to rigen. n
fact we do not have all of his writings erome’s notice at   ir. ll .  implies
that there were a large number. o their contents we have no clue, but
uasten’s criticism of the smallness of his   oeuvre   seems unjusti fi ed.
 he   anarion
piphanius’ major effort is very long, and was divided by its author,   fi rst
into three ooks and then into seven ections. oll’s edition, with notes and
apparatus, occupies about  pages. t was begun in  or  ( anarion
 roem  ,) and produced in great haste, in less than three years. ook ,
translated here, etends through ect  and comprises somewhat more
than a third of the whole.
he   anarion   is an heresiology that is, it is a work which describes bodies,
systems and views which the author regards as subversive of true religion and
presents his arguments against them. he genre is found in the hristian,
the uslim and some oriental traditions, and is alive today. n piphanius’
time it was well established his ook  is deeply indebted to ippolytus and
renaeus, both of whom had ustin artyr for a predecessor. piphanius in
his turn served as a source for heodoret and others.
nusually for an ancient author, piphanius titled his own work. t the
very outset he eplains this title and its meaning and lays out the plan and
purpose of his book
(roem  ,-) . . .  am writing you a preface to give the gist of my
treatise against sects. ince  shall be telling you the names of the
sects and eposing their unlawful deeds like poisons and toic sub-
stances, matching the antidotes with them at the same time—cures
for those who are already bitten, and preventatives for those who will
have this misfortune— am drafting this reface here for the scholarly,
to eplain the “anarion”, or chest of remedies for the victims of wild
beasts’ bites. t is a work in three olumes and contains eighty ects,
which answer symbolically to wild animals or snakes.
   introduction

piphanius’ intent then is to convert and to protect. is means of doing so
is to identify wrong doctrines so that his fellow hristians can keep away
from them, and to convince of the truth those who have stumbled into these
doctrines. e has been called an “heresy hunter” but the term scarcely
epresses what he meant to do. t has also been pointed out that he was
following conventions which by his time had been   fi ed. hile this is so,
his vehemence makes it plain that he was not merely falling in with some
established pattern he meant every word of what he wrote.
n his second roem he eplains what he means by “antidotes”
(roem  ,) “nd to correspond with these (serpents and beasts) 
shall give as many arguments, like antidotes, as  can in short com-
pass—one or two at most—to counteract their poison and, after the
ord, to save anyone who cares to be, when he has willingly or
inadvertently fallen into these snakelike teachings of the sects.”
hese quotations will show that piphanius is writing, not simply of heretical
ideas as such but of heretical ideas in the contet of the sects which hold and

would be easy to assemble, from the writings of patrologists and historians

his manner of holding it intransigent. bove all he vehemently opposed the
teachings of the great commentator rigen, the   fi rst hristian systematic
theologian and as a thinker far superior to piphanius.
s to the last charge, rigen had many opponents piphanius only
commanded the widest audience. urther, he admired some of rigen’s
achievements his attack on him was not that of an obscurantist on an
intellectual but that of a doctrinal purist on a teacher whom many considered
heretical.
s to the epithet, “nasty,” name calling was characteristic of controversial
writings in the fourth century, though it must be admitted that piphanius
carried it to etremes. e in fact apologizes for this in his   fi rst roem. he
terms of abuse he uses, he says, are his way of distancing himself from

the objects of his least pleasant epithets with others he can be a little more
polite.
o the charge of uncritical judgment, an advocate of piphanius must

the testimony he wanted to believe. hen he had direct contact with his

punished (for indulging) in moral sins, how much more when we sin
because of false doctrines or if a life of good morals suf fi ced men
for salvation, why is it that many philosophers among the gentiles who
live continently, and many among the heretics, can by no means be
saved, as if the falseness of their doctrine obscured and sullied their
manner of life

promises his correspondents “neither to refuse nor to postpone” his answer

(nc. ,) the teachings of the divine, sacred scripture with regard
to the salvation which is among us, the   fi rm foundation of our faith
concerning ather, on and oly pirit and all the rest of the salva-

the advent in the   fl esh of the nly-begotten, concerning both the

o these imperatives piphanius devoted his life. is controversial writings
are intended to teach them and to defend them from attacks which he
considered perverse and dangerous.
s we have tried to show, the student of ag ammadi and other nostic
literature needs piphanius and his fellow heresiologists in order to see the
full picture of what was at stake. eyond this, a church historian or historian
of religion has several reasons for consulting this writer. s is well known he
preserves documents which are not available elsewhere and is an important
witness to the reek tet of renaeus and the events of the fourth century,
in which he was a participant.
or another reason the historian needs to know something of him. is is
the fourth century voice of what in our day we would call “fundamentalism.”
autin has said of him, Épiphane sera resté jusqu’à son dernier souf fl e un
moine égyptien. ersons and schools of piphanius’ kind have always had
great in fl uence, not only throughout hristian history but throughout that
of all the great religions. o understand the past, and therefore the present,
it is as necessary to know them as it is to know the great creative thinkers.

letter written in the ninety-second year of the iocletian era, the twelfth
of the reign of alentinian and alens and the eighth of ratian,   to
piphanius of leutheropolis in alestine, some time abbot in the
country about leutheropolis, now bishop of the city of onstantia in the
province of yprus, from the presbyters cacius and aul, archimandrites,
that is, abbots in halcis and eroea in oelesyria. hey requested that
he write a complete heresiology and not only they, but many others
as well, urged and practically compelled him to take up the task.
reetings in the ord from the archimandrites, cacius, presbyter, and
aul, presbyter, to the most godly ather, the bishop piphanius, our master
and most highly honored in every way.
,    glimpse of your everence would suf fi ce us, ather, by   fi lling
us with spiritual speech and implanting as much affection in us as has
arisen in those who enjoy your acquaintance. () ut by its heralding of
the fragrance of the sweet odor of his words and deeds, fame, which runs
before a disciple of the avior, presses one to take one’s   fi  ll of his words
and thought. e ought to have come in person to partake of the grace
which od has given to you, as to the apostles.
,   ut since the journey is prohibited by bodily in fi rmity and distress,
we are unable to come ourselves, fall prostrate at your feet, and hear and
learn the sacred, spiritual words as they issue from your lips. () (or we
are con fi dent that if we came and heard them, were we worthy, we would
be set upon the way of life we have undertaken—provided that we are   fi t
to attain its goal.)
,   ince in fi rmity has overtaken us, therefore, we beseech your ever-
ence in all your greatness not to grudge sharing with us the gifts you have
truly been given by the avior. () or not we alone, but all who hear of
you, confess that the avior has raised you up in this generation as a new
apostle and herald, a new ohn, to proclaim the things that ought to be
observed by those who have undertaken this course.
,   s arcellus, a brother to us both, is pressed by your fame in its
greatness and drawn by affection for your everence, and since he is a

 member of our community, we have employed his services, although he
is a recent catechumen, for the making of such a long journey, and have
committed to him the venture, in all its daring, of us sinners towards you,
the avior’s disciple. () nd our request is that you give us, for our instruc-
tion, some of the words you have spoken to certain brethren. or you, the
righteous, this can be no burden but for us sinners it will be rejoicing in
the ord when we partake of them for the load of our transgressions is
lightened when we are   fi lled with your spiritual uttterances. () e have
heard names assigned to the sects by your onor, and are asking your
everence to tell us eplicitly the heresy held by each of these cults. or
not everyone’s gift is the same.
,   e likewise ask you, the righteous, to pray to the ord for all who
long for you and are awaiting the gift from you. () e are in fasting and
prayer that the brother of us all may be received gladly by your onor
and obtain the gift of your bestowing, and so offer the accustomed prayers
to ather, on and oly pirit.
,   ll the brethren hope to be established by your prayer on their
behalf. ince yours is a od-given grace of apostles we urge you to share it
ungrudgingly. () ll the little ones in the cloisters are praying the greater
that they may enjoy a spiritual gift from your everence. ay you remain
well in the ord, and happy in hrist and the oly pirit as you admin-
ister the throne that has been granted you and your od-given gift, till
you receive the crown that awaits you.

piphanius’ reply to the presbyters cacius and aul, concerning their
letter to him about his writing an heresiology. (roem )
reetings in the ord from piphanius to his highly esteemed brothers
and fellow-presbyters, asters cacius and aul!
,   y drawing up a preface or opening statement as a sort of title,
authors of old would give a glimpse, by means of the hint, of the entire
work that followed. n the same style, beloved,  too am writing a preface
for you, to give a brief summary of my  treatise  against sects. () ince
 am going to tell you the names of the sects and epose their unlawful
deeds like poisons and toic substances, and at the same time match the
antidotes with them as cures for those already bitten and preventatives
for those who will have this misfortune,  am drafting this reface for the
scholarly to eplain the “anarion,” or chest of remedies for those whom
savage beasts have bitten. t is composed in three ooks containing eighty
ects, symbolically represented by wild beasts or snakes.
,   ut “one after the eighty” is at once the foundation, teaching
and saving treatment of the truth and hrist’s holy bride, the church. t
has always been but was revealed in the course of time, through hrist’s
incarnation, in the midst of these sects. ()  have made mention it in
connection with the preaching of hrist and again, after all the iniquities
of these sects, given a concise, clear account of it in accordance with the
apostles’ teaching, for the refreshment of those who by reading have labored
their way through the sects.
,   lease, all you scholarly readers of the reface, the ects that fol-
low it, and the efense of the ruth and position of the ruth and the
aith of the oly atholic hurch pardon me—who am only human
and am trying my best, with hard labor and od-given zeal, to defend the
true religion,—() if   attempt  too much in my desire to make the best
defense in my power, in the all-holy, all-august ame itself. or od allows me
this, though  am investigating matters too dif fi cult for me, since what  say
is for the truth’s sake, and my work is for the sake of true religion.
,   nd  further beg your  pardon  if you should   fi nd—though it is
certainly not my way to mock or ridicule people—but if, from zeal against
the sects and for the readers’ dissuasion,  may speak in anger or call certain
persons “frauds,” or “tramps” or “wretches.” () he very necessity for the

proem i
 words of the controversy is putting me in such a sweat, for the readers’
dissuasion and to show that these persons’ practices, rites and doctrines
are the furthest thing from my mind, and thus prove my independence of
them with the words and the bitterness of my opposition, and turn people
away from them precisely by the words that appear too harsh.
,   nd here are the contents of the entire work in its three olumes,
olumes ne, wo, and hree—which three olumes  have divided into
seven ections with a certain number of ects and chisms in each section,
making eighty in all. heir names and the occasions of them, are these
() he   fi rst, arbarism. he second, cythianism. he third, ellenism.
he fourth, udaism. he   fi fth, amaritanism. () erived from these are
the following. efore hrist’s incarnation, but after arbarism and the
cythian superstition, the sects which sprang from ellenism are these
he sith, ythagoreans or eripatetics, a sect which was separated (from
ellenism) by ristotle. he seventh, latonists. he eighth, toics. he
ninth, picureans.
,   hen the amaritan sect, an offshoot of udaism, and its four
peoples he tenth, orothenes. he eleventh, ebuaeans. he twelfth,
ssenes. he thirteenth, ositheans.
,   hen the afore-mentioned udaism itself, which derived its character
from braham, was ampli fi ed through the aw given to oses, and inher-
ited its name, “udaism,” from udah the son of acob or srael, through
avid, the king from the tribe of udah. () nd derived from udaism
itself are the following seven sects he fourteenth, cribes. he   fi fteenth,
harisees. he siteenth, adducees. he seventeenth, emerobaptists.
he eighteenth, ssaeans. he nineteenth, asaraeans. he twentieth,
erodians.
,   rom these sects, and later on in the course of time, appeared the
saving dispensation of our ord esus hrist—that is to say, his incarnation,
preaching of the ospel, and proclamation of a kingdom. his alone is
the fount of salvation, and the faith in the truth of the catholic, apostolic,
and orthodo church.
,   rom this the following sects, which have hrist’s name only but
not his faith, have been broken away and split off () he   fi rst, imonians.
he second, enandrians. he third, atornilians. he fourth, asilideans.
